ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for seafood pesto pasta; closest parent 'pasta dish' (FOODON_00002473) used as anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of seafood pesto pasta is approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of cooked pasta tossed with pesto sauce, prawns, fish, tomatoes, and mushrooms.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pasta with seafood composite; supplemented with USDA SR Legacy entries for pesto sauce, cooked pasta, prawns, and fish fillet
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for seafood pesto pasta exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by weighted-ingredient composition using USDA SR Legacy data for: cooked spaghetti/linguine (~50% by weight), cooked prawns (~15%), cooked white fish fillet (~10%), basil pesto sauce (~15%), tomatoes (~5%), and mushrooms (~5%). Pesto sauce nutrient profile derived from USDA FDC entry 'Pesto sauce' (FDC ID 172944). Energy cross-checked against USDA composite pasta-with-seafood entries.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta (cooked, al dente) as analogous food; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for seafood pesto pasta as a composite dish. GI of 45 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: cooked pasta (spaghetti/linguine, al dente), which has a published GI of approximately 40–50 (mean ~45) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Diabetes Care). The pesto sauce, seafood, and vegetables contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates and are expected to lower the effective GI slightly via fat and protein co-ingestion. GL per serving calculated as: (45 × 64.75 g available carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 29.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~1085 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be cautious with this dish as a single restaurant serving provides approximately 1085 mg sodium (~47% of the 2300 mg daily limit); requesting reduced salt or sauce on the side can meaningfully lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load with low-GI pasta base (GI ~45)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The al dente pasta base provides a low-to-moderate glycemic index (~45), but the total carbohydrate load per serving (~65 g) yields a glycemic load of approximately 29, which is high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from seafood and monounsaturated fats from olive oil in pesto
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of prawns, fish, and olive oil-based pesto provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids and monounsaturated fats; saturated fat content is relatively low (~1.3 g/100 g), making this dish broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet when sodium is managed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seafood Pesto Pasta is a Western-style pasta dish adapted for the Singapore restaurant market, featuring cooked pasta (typically spaghetti or linguine) tossed in a homemade basil pesto sauce made with fresh basil, pine nuts or mixed nuts, olive oil, garlic, and parmesan cheese. The dish is topped with prawns and fish fillets, complemented by cherry tomatoes and mushrooms. The texture is al dente pasta coated in a rich, herbaceous, and slightly nutty pesto sauce, with tender seafood providing a savoury, oceanic flavour contrast. Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate energy density (~168 kcal/100 g), meaningful protein from the seafood (~9.8 g/100 g), and heart-healthy monounsaturated fats from olive oil. The pasta base contributes a low-to-moderate glycemic index (~45), though the total glycemic load per restaurant serving is high (~29) due to portion size. Sodium content is elevated (~310 mg/100 g) from the pesto sauce and seasoning, making sodium management important for individuals with hypertension. The dish contains major allergens including gluten (pasta), crustaceans (prawns), fish, tree nuts (pesto), and dairy (parmesan). It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal-certified food without specific restaurant confirmation. This dish is commonly served as a main course at Western-style cafes and casual dining restaurants in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in seafood pesto pasta?
Is seafood pasta good for diabetics?
Is pesto pasta high in carbs?
Can I eat pesto pasta with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for seafood pesto pasta exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional food composition database. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-ingredient composition using USDA SR Legacy and FoodData Central approved sources. GI is estimated from the analogous cooked pasta GI per University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is systematic, ingredient-based, and cross-referenced against multiple USDA entries.
notes: 1. Cuisine tag in input JSON was 'Coffee', which is inconsistent with the dish description; the canonical cuisine has been corrected to 'Western' based on cuisine_tags array which includes 'Western' and 'Pasta'. 2. The emoji '🥜' in the original food_name has been removed to produce a clean canonical English name. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA composite estimation for this dish type. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed negligible/trace per USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients (olive oil, pasta, seafood). 5. The dish contains tree nuts (pine nuts or mixed nuts) in the pesto sauce as explicitly stated in the source description; this is a critical allergen flag. 6. Halal status is unconfirmed as the restaurant (The Tree Cafe - Funan) is not listed as a halal-certified establishment; is_halal is set to false pending confirmation. 7. Serving size of 350 g is estimated based on typical Singapore restaurant pasta portion sizes; no exact weight was provided by the source. 8. GL per serving = (GI 45 × carbohydrate_g per serving 64.75) / 100 = 29.1. Carbohydrate per serving = 18.5 g/100 g × 350 g = 64.75 g. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and all micronutrients except vitamin_d_mcg are estimated from USDA component data and are not confirmed zero; vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this composite.
